items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.correct: Five distinct arguments for God's existence: from motion, causation, contingency, gradation of being, and final causes/teleology — collectively forming Aquinas's natural theology, not a single argument.
items.misconception: Aquinas's Five Ways are five steps of one cosmological argument.
items.random_wrong: Aquinas's Five Ways are five Christian sacraments.
items.why: Each Way is a self-standing argument with different structure. Students treat them as a single chain, missing that Aquinas offered multiple independent routes — the failure of one doesn't undermine the others.

items.q: What does natural law theory base morality on?
items.correct: Human nature and reason — there are objective purposes (telos) built into humans which reason can discover, generating universal moral precepts.
items.misconception: Natural law means whatever happens in nature is morally right.
items.random_wrong: Natural law is the same thing as the laws of physics.
items.why: The 'naturalistic fallacy' confusion — students assume natural law endorses lions eating gazelles or natural disasters. Aquinas's theory concerns human nature and rational ends, not all natural events.

items.q: Does the cosmological argument say 'everything has a cause'?
items.correct: No — its premise is that whatever is moved, caused, or contingent depends on another; a necessary, uncaused God is not an exception to that premise but its terminus.
items.misconception: The argument states that absolutely everything has a cause — so God would need a cause as well.
items.random_wrong: The argument states that nothing has a cause.
items.why: 'What caused God?' misfires against Aquinas because the premise never said everything has a cause. Setting up the premise accurately is essential before evaluating the argument.

items.q: Does Big Bang cosmology settle the cosmological argument?
items.correct: No — Aquinas's versions concern ongoing dependence, not just temporal beginnings (he conceded philosophy cannot prove the world began); the Big Bang is most relevant to the Kalam variant.
items.misconception: The Big Bang proves the cosmological argument because it shows the universe had a beginning.
items.random_wrong: The Big Bang disproves the cosmological argument by showing the universe caused itself.
items.why: Students treat 'first cause' as 'first event in time'. For Aquinas the First Cause sustains the series here and now; whether the past is finite is a separate question taken up by Craig's Kalam argument.

items.q: Aquinas' first Way is an argument from what?
items.why: Correct answer: Motion. “Design” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is Aquinas' term for God as 'pure act'?
items.why: It's Actus purus. “Ens necessarium” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many Ways does Aquinas present in the Summa Theologica?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
